exemplar.title: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Maintains a sustained and clear focus on the prescribed title throughout the essay
Effectively integrates two areas of knowledge (the arts and the natural sciences) with a coherent temporal framework
Uses a range of well-chosen historical and contemporary examples to support arguments
Demonstrates awareness of alternative viewpoints and signals implications of paradigm shifts
weaknesses: Analysis often remains at a descriptive level and does not consistently probe underlying TOK concepts such as authority or methodological rigor
Reliance on tertiary sources (e.g. Wikipedia) weakens conceptual precision
Counter-arguments are acknowledged but not systematically evaluated
Conclusion restates the thesis without fully exploring broader implications for future knowledge production
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The strand assessment placed this essay in the 5â6 band: it is focused on the title, linked effectively to areas of knowledge, and supported by clear examples, with some awareness of different perspectives. However, the uneven depth of analysis, occasional descriptiveness, and reliance on tertiary sources prevent it from reaching the 7â8 band. Given the official examinerâs total mark of 6, the essay merits the maximum in the 5â6 band, i.e. 6/10, reflecting its solid but not exceptional TOK development.

annotations.comment: The feminist art movement example is timely and persuasive; however, the student should explicitly discuss how gender biases have historically influenced which art is considered authoritative.
The phrase âknowledge is inseparable from the time it is createdâ is thought-provoking; linking this to the concept of historical bias would strengthen the TOK discussion.
The conclusion restates the thesis but lacks critical evaluation of broader implications, such as how fragility of knowledge affects trust in scientific and artistic communities.
To enhance critical evaluation, the student could compare the rejection of Wegenerâs theory with modern criteria for theory acceptance, such as peer review and empirical testing.
Consider clarifying how empirical anomalies challenge established models, making revered scientific theories susceptible to revision.
The student should formulate an explicit knowledge question from the prompt to guide the analysis, clarifying how it frames the exploration of fragility in revered knowledge.
It would benefit the essay to include a brief outline of the structure, signaling how each area of knowledge will be examined to enhance coherence.

The definition of ârevered knowledgeâ relies heavily on a tertiary source and remains abstract; the student should integrate TOK terminology (e.g., authority, paradigm) to deepen conceptual clarity.
The temporal approach is a strong organizational framework, demonstrating clear awareness of how socio-historical context shapes knowledge over time.

To deepen analysis, the student could formulate an explicit knowledge question, such as âTo what extent do paradigm shifts reveal the fragility of our most revered knowledge?,â to anchor the discussion.
The Spanish Colonial art example introduces historical change but remains descriptive; the student should examine how power and authority influenced the artâs initial reverence.
The transition into the art example is abrupt and the sentence is incomplete; ensure full articulation of ideas and smoother linkage between concepts.

This paragraph summarizes shifts in perception of Spanish Colonial art but lacks evaluation of implications for knowledge production; the student should explore how changing values reshape accepted truths.
The Impressionism case is a compelling historical illustration of paradigm shift, effectively demonstrating initial resistance and eventual acceptance of new artistic knowledge.
Building on this example, the student should explicitly link the criticsâ authority and methodological norms to TOK concepts, such as expert consensus and aesthetic justification.
The student acknowledges alternative perspectives but does not consistently evaluate them; consider examining why initial rejection of Impressionism reveals epistemic tensions.
The statement about perception change is generic; refine the thesis to specify how each chosen example exemplifies fragility in revered knowledge.
This introduction to natural sciences effectively positions the next examples, but could be strengthened by referencing scientific methods and their role in knowledge evaluation.
The Lamarckism example is informative but descriptive; the student should analyze how criteria of evidence and reproducibility expose the theoryâs scientific fragility.
The continental drift discussion is detailed; linking this to Kuhnâs concept of scientific revolutions would deepen the TOK analysis of paradigm shifts.
The discussion of street art is a strong contemporary example; the student should connect its momentary nature to TOK notions of temporal bias and the fragility of knowledge artifacts.
When contrasting traditional gallery art with graffiti, the student could evaluate the role of institutional authority in determining what knowledge is revered or dismissed.
This summary sentence is too general; the student should specify how methodological advances precipitated the acceptance of scientific theories, linking back to fragility.
Incorporating TOK perspectives on power structures and ethics would strengthen analysis of how feminist art redefines criteria for reverence in artistic knowledge.

The comparison of generational responses to the âArsenic Lifeâ hypothesis is insightful; to deepen the critique, analyze how methodological rigor and peer review processes influenced its rejection.
This transitional summary is broad; the student should synthesize how each example collectively supports the thesis rather than merely restating cases.
Consider addressing potential counter-claims to your conclusion to demonstrate evaluation and deepen the essayâs critical rigor.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
